curator: auto (generate_day.py — reals verified verbatim vs source + cross-validated)

quotes.text: I told the label, 'I need three things on this tour: a tuner, a spare guitar, and a woman who finds feedback erotic.' They gave me a tuner. One out of three is a very corporate answer.
quotes.speaker: Jimi Hendrix
quotes.context: Press roundtable, Melody Maker offices, London, September 1968 — cited in Charles Shaar Murray's 'Crosstown Traffic' (1989)
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Hendrix did talk about his guitar in frankly sensual terms and had a wry, deadpan wit that's often overlooked, so the setup rings true. The giveaway is 'very corporate answer' — that's MBA-era vocabulary, not 1968 Hendrix.

quotes.text: People say you shouldn't eat a full rack of ribs before a two-hour show. Those people are not comedians. Those people are cowards. The belch alone cleared the front row and they laughed harder than anyone.
quotes.speaker: Sam Kinison
quotes.context: Q&A session following a warm-up set at the Comedy Store, Sunset Strip, Los Angeles, recorded for a 1988 Showtime special behind-the-scenes reel
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Kinison's entire brand was gleeful, physical transgression, and the audience-as-collateral-damage framing is very him. The giveaway is 'Those people are cowards' — that specific construction was more an '80s action-movie line than Kinison's actual vocabulary, which ran more biblical.

quotes.text: Health nuts are going to feel stupid someday, lying in hospitals dying of nothing.
quotes.speaker: Redd Foxx
quotes.context: A signature dark one-liner from the pioneering, famously raunchy stand-up comedian (star of 'Sanford and Son'), mocking clean-living health fanatics. Foxx's party-hard, cigarette-and-booze persona was legendary.

source.title: Quote Details: Redd Foxx (The Quotations Page)

quotes.text: My rider said two bottles of Bordeaux in the dressing room. They sent up Beaujolais. I didn't throw the TV out the window — I threw the Beaujolais out the window and drank the TV. I don't know what that means. I was very angry.
quotes.speaker: Oliver Reed
quotes.context: The Russell Harty Show, ITV, recorded March 1974 — quoted in Robert Sellers' biography 'Hellraisers' (2008)
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Reed's chat-show chaos is the stuff of legend and the non-sequitur 'drank the TV' pivot is exactly his drunk-surrealist logic. The subtle giveaway is the self-aware 'I don't know what that means' — Reed was usually too committed to the bit to break the fourth wall like that.

quotes.text: I don't go on stage high but I'll take an edible after I get off stage and chill out. I do write a lot of jokes while high though, and then the next day I'll look at them and go, 'Well that's not that funny.'
quotes.speaker: Jim Jefferies
quotes.context: Crude Australian comedian Jim Jefferies — who built his name on drinking, drugs, and porn material — talking candidly about weed and his creative process, in a High Times interview (Aug 2023).

source.title: Jim Jefferies Is Living His Best Life (High Times)

quotes.text: Alcohol was my poison. I drank Seagrams VO, a Canadian rye, with Coca-Cola. And beer. For me, beer was like air. I'd have a six-pack before noon.
quotes.speaker: Alice Cooper
quotes.context: Shock-rock legend Alice Cooper describing his heavy daily drinking before he got sober, in a Louder/Classic Rock feature on going cold turkey.

source.title: How Alice Cooper went cold turkey and made his most personal album (Louder)
